notes: George P. Noon (MD Baylor 1960) began his surgical residency at Baylor in 1960 under DeBakey and joined the faculty in 1965. The Baylor Department of Surgery History of Leadership page documents that Noon 'brought great distinction to Baylor College of Medicine beginning in 1956, when he was a medical student, and to the Department of Surgery since 1965.' In 1968, Noon and DeBakey performed among the first heart transplants in the US. In 1988, Noon and DeBakey met with NASA engineers to develop the miniature axial flow blood pump (MicroMed DeBakey-Noon VAD). Noon supervised the Yeltsin bypass surgery alongside DeBakey in 1996. Over 350 publications. The Noon-DeBakey partnership represents one of the longest surgeon-mentor collaborations in American cardiovascular surgery history (1960-2008). TARGET MODULE: 04_cardiothoracic_vascular.json

Kenneth L. Mattox (b. 1938, MD Baylor 1964) trained in general and thoracic surgery at Baylor under DeBakey and became professor and vice chairman of surgery in the Michael E. DeBakey Department of Surgery. He serves as chief of staff and chief of surgery at Ben Taub General Hospital (Harris County's Level I trauma center). Mattox authored the definitive personal tribute 'Michael Ellis DeBakey, MD: Texas Physician Personified' in Texas Medicine (2008), written from the perspective of a trainee and career-long colleague. He is internationally recognized as a leader in trauma surgery and organized the annual Trauma, Critical Care and Acute Care Surgery conference for decades. This edge connects the DeBakey cardiovascular hub to the trauma surgery network. TARGET MODULE: 04_cardiothoracic_vascular.json

George C. Morris Jr. (MD University of Pennsylvania 1948) served as 'the first surgical resident under Dr. Michael E. DeBakey in 1950' per the Baylor Department of Surgery History of Leadership page. DeBakey stated that 'George Morris was one of the best surgeons he ever trained.' Morris became a pioneer in peripheral vascular surgery at Baylor and published extensively with DeBakey on arterial reconstruction. He served as chief of surgery at the DeBakey VA Medical Center. As DeBakey's first resident at Baylor, Morris represents the origin point of the Baylor surgical training lineage. TARGET MODULE: 04_cardiothoracic_vascular.json

H. Edward Garrett Sr. 'joined the Department of Surgery faculty after completing his training in general and thoracic surgery at Baylor College of Medicine under the direction of Dr. Michael E. DeBakey' per the Baylor History of Leadership page. In 1964, Garrett performed the first successful coronary artery bypass graft and subsequently published the first series of bypass operations for lower leg arteries. This operation — arguably the most consequential procedure in modern cardiovascular surgery — was performed by a DeBakey trainee using techniques developed under DeBakey's mentorship. Exact residency start date approximated from career timeline. TARGET MODULE: 04_cardiothoracic_vascular.json

Oscar Creech Jr. (MD Jefferson Medical College 1941, residency Tulane) joined DeBakey at Baylor University College of Medicine in 1949, one of DeBakey's earliest recruits along with Cooley and Morris. Cooley's memorial for DeBakey (Tex Heart Inst J 2008) names Creech among the founding Baylor surgery staff: 'His early staff included me and Drs. Oscar Creech, B.W. Haynes, and John Howard.' Creech served at Baylor until 1956, then returned to Tulane University as professor and chair of the Department of Surgery, thereby extending the DeBakey lineage back to the Ochsner/Tulane trunk from which DeBakey himself originated — creating a Hopkins-like bidirectional circuit (Ochsner trained DeBakey at Tulane; DeBakey trained Creech at Baylor; Creech returned to lead Tulane). TARGET MODULE: 04_cardiothoracic_vascular.json
